testcase.name: 0200-table-parameters-testcase
Table Parameters Testcase
testcase.description: Table linkbase test case using table parameters

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters
variation.description: Simple table with two rows (primary items m1, m2) and two columns (explicit dimension D = d1, d2). The primary items are specified by referencing formula parameters directly (i.e. not as table parameters). In this variation there is no instance

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters, with instance
variation.description: Simple table with two rows (primary items m1, m2) and two columns (explicit dimension D = d1, d2). The primary items are specified by referencing formula parameters directly (i.e. not as table parameters).

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters that require an instance
variation.description: Simple table with two rows (primary items m1, m2) and two columns (explicit dimension D = d1, d2). The dimension members are specified by referencing formula parameters that extract QNames from the instance. In this variation there is no instance, so the table cannot be rendered.

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters that require an instance, with instance
variation.description: Simple table with two rows (primary items m1, m2) and two columns (explicit dimension D = d1, d2). The dimension members are specified by referencing formula parameters that extract QNames from the instance.

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters and using table-parameter relationships to form a table set
variation.description: Simple table with one row (primary items $pi) and one column (explicit dimension D = $dm). Both $pi and $dm are derived from formula parameters linked to the table definition by table-parameter relationships, and {m1, m2} x {d1, d2} gives four tables from the one definition.

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters and using table-parameter relationships to form a table set (with instance)
variation.description: Simple table with one row (primary items $pi) and one column (explicit dimension D = $dm). Both $pi and $dm are derived from formula parameters linked to the table definition by table-parameter relationships, and {m1, m2} x {d1, d2} gives four tables from the one definition.

variation.name: Table referencing formula parameters, one of which evaluates to an empty sequence
variation.description: The empty sequence should mean that the Cartesian product of the parameters is empty and thus no tables are produced.

variation.name: Table set where the table parameters' Cartesian product contains duplicate mappings
variation.description: Cartesian product produces (m1, d1) and (m1, d1). No de-duplication is specified, so two tables are expected.
